words.example: Wildfires abound in the lush green meadows of the valley.
words.mnemonic: A-bound -> ABUNDance. Think of how heat transfer problems abound in thermodynamics; they are present everywhere in large quantities.

words.example: His plans for the future were still amorphous, changing every few weeks.
words.mnemonic: 'A' (no) + 'morph' (shape). Like a liquid metal before casting, it has no defined structure.

words.definition: (of a person) strict and stern in character; (of living conditions) lacking luxury; extremely harsh
words.example: With his austere manner, Mr. Thompson rarely cracked a smile. / John moved into an austere apartment with only the most basic furnishings and no luxuries.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'aansoo' (tears) + 'tear'. Or think of a very strict lab instructor who gives zero marks for formatting errors—a very austere person.

words.definition: give a false impression of; mask
words.example: Her sparkling eyes belie the sadness she must feel.
words.mnemonic: 'Be' + 'lie'. To be a lie. A perfectly smooth surface finish might belie the massive internal stresses hiding inside a machined part.

words.example: The capricious nature of the weather has made planning outdoor events difficult.
words.mnemonic: Think of the unpredictable, capricious nature of the weather in Ahmedabad during the monsoon—sunny one minute, raining the next.

words.definition: related to the brain; intellectual; focused on learning rather than emotion
words.example: The surgeon specialized in cerebral procedures. / Sophia had a cerebral approach to solving problems.
words.mnemonic: The 'cerebrum' is a part of the brain. Deriving numerical solutions for complex ODEs requires a highly cerebral approach rather than an emotional one.

words.definition: (of a person) likable and friendly, often due to shared interests; (of a thing) enjoyable because it aligns with one's tendencies
words.example: Congenial as she was, Nancy was always invited to parties. / She found the small town a more congenial way of life.
words.mnemonic: 'Con' (together) + 'genial' (gene). Having similar genes means you are friendly. Hanging out at the campus cafeteria with friends who share your goals is a congenial environment.

words.example: On Insta, it's important that your vacation be as conspicuous as possible.
words.mnemonic: 'Con' + 'spic' (see). Can be easily seen. Wearing a bright orange safety helmet on a factory floor makes you very conspicuous.

words.example: He gave a cursory glance at the document before signing it.
words.mnemonic: Think of moving a computer 'cursor' across a screen rapidly without reading. Doing a cursory check on a lab report before submitting usually leaves silly mistakes behind.

words.definition: scary or frightening; (of a task) seeming impossible
words.example: Memorizing 960 GRE words is daunting but necessary.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Don'. Facing a mafia Don is scary. Similarly, designing and analyzing a full monocoque chassis from scratch can feel like a daunting task.

words.definition: look up to someone or something as a god
words.example: In some ancient cultures, the sun was deified and worshiped.
words.mnemonic: From 'Deity' (god). To make someone a deity. In India, fans often deify famous cricket players.

words.definition: meant for teaching; educational, sometimes in a condescending manner
words.example: The play was not only entertaining but also didactic, offering lessons on morality.
words.mnemonic: 'Didi' (elder sister) + 'act'. Didi always acts like a teacher, giving you didactic (and sometimes annoying) advice.

words.example: The invention of the printing press allowed information to disseminate rapidly.
words.mnemonic: 'Semin' means seed. To scatter seeds everywhere. A good business plan needs marketing to disseminate the idea to the public.

words.example: Hopefully carbon capture technology will be feasible in the future.
words.mnemonic: 'Fee' + 'sible'. It is possible to pay the fee. In industry, before launching a product, you must check if the manufacturing process is economically feasible.

words.example: Students flouted the classroom's 'no chewing gum' rule.
words.mnemonic: Rhymes with 'out'. You get kicked OUT if you flout the rules. Like intentionally ignoring safety protocols in a workshop.

words.example: The population in the small town was quite homogeneous, with little diversity.
words.mnemonic: 'Homo' (same) + 'geneous' (kind). Think of a perfectly mixed alloy where the material properties are exactly the same everywhere.

words.example: More often than not, Netflix movies are humdrum, cookie-cutter affairs.
words.mnemonic: 'Hum' + 'drum'. Listening to the exact same hum and drum beat gets boring. The repetitive hum of a generator in the background can be very humdrum.

words.example: The professor was tiring of the students' insipid remarks.
words.mnemonic: 'In' + 'sip'. You take a sip and there's no flavor. Like drinking plain hot water when you were expecting a strong masala chai.

words.definition: likely to talk a lot; wordy
words.example: Loquacious to a fault, Bill never took the hint to, on occasion, shut up.
words.mnemonic: 'Loqua' is a root word for speech. Someone who is loquacious is like that one classmate who won't stop chattering during a lecture.

words.definition: having a distaste for humans and society
words.example: No one has seen that misanthropic hermit in years.
words.mnemonic: 'Mis' (hate/wrong) + 'anthro' (mankind, like anthropology). A misanthropic person hates being around people.

words.example: The authoritarian regime likes to call itself a 'democracy,' an obvious misnomer.
words.mnemonic: 'Mis' (wrong) + 'nomer' (name). Calling a standard two-wheeler scooter a 'heavy-duty all-terrain vehicle' is a huge misnomer.

words.example: The captain's negligent behavior led to the death of thousands.
words.mnemonic: Directly related to 'neglect'. Being negligent with scheduled machine maintenance will eventually lead to major breakdowns.

words.example: Dictators tend to surround themselves with obsequious apologists.
words.mnemonic: 'Obse' (obsessed) + 'qui' (yes). Someone obsessed with saying 'yes' to the boss just to get favors.

words.definition: decrease someone's anger or other negative emotion
words.example: He tried to placate the angry customer by offering a full refund.
words.mnemonic: Give a 'plate' of 'cake' to a crying child to placate them and calm them down.

words.definition: a fondness or liking for something
words.example: Her proclivity for expensive pastries is wreaking havoc on her finances.
words.mnemonic: 'Pro' (forward) + 'cliff'. A tendency to lean forward. Having a proclivity for entrepreneurship means you naturally lean toward exploring business ideas.

words.example: The mature audience was unimpressed by the comedian's puerile humor.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Purely ill'. Throwing chalk across a college classroom is puerile behavior.

words.example: His quixotic dream of creating a world without conflict seemed impossible.
words.mnemonic: 'Quick' + 'exotic'. Having exotic, unrealistic dreams. Planning to build a fully functional flying car over a single weekend is a quixotic idea.

words.example: He was a spendthrift, constantly buying unnecessary items and racking up debt.
words.mnemonic: 'Spends' all their 'thrift' (savings). Buying a heavily overpriced sports bike when a simple Activa gets the job done easily is the mark of a spendthrift.

words.example: He's generally taciturn, so when he speaks you know it's important.
words.mnemonic: 'Taxi' + 'turn'. A taxi driver who just takes the turns and drives you to your destination without speaking a single word.

words.example: We can't always be wary; sometimes we have to take risks.
words.mnemonic: Think of 'War'. In a war zone, you must be very wary of your surroundings. Just like you must be wary around high-voltage equipment.

words.example: Chemical runoff regularly adulterates our water supply.
words.mnemonic: Adding cheaper, weaker metals to a high-quality alloy will adulterate (corrupt) its structural integrity.

words.definition: support; be in favor of / someone who supports a cause, organization, or person
words.example: The board advocated a more conservative marketing strategy. / John is an advocate for many progressive causes.
words.mnemonic: 'Ad' + 'vocal'. Being vocal about adding a new policy or idea means you support it.

words.example: Roman leading men never passed on an opportunity to aggrandize themselves.
words.mnemonic: Contains the word 'Grand'. To make your business or your reputation seem much grander and more powerful than it is.

words.example: The children completed their chores with surprising alacrity.
words.mnemonic: Sounds a bit like 'electricity'. When you have an entrepreneurial idea you love, you get charged up and jump into it with electricity and eagerness.

words.example: It's normal to feel ambivalent when moving to a new place.
words.mnemonic: 'Ambi' means both (like ambidextrous). You have feelings for *both* sides of a decision, leaving you unsure which to pick.

words.definition: improve a bad feeling or bad situation
words.example: Strong service and timely support can ameliorate customer anger.
words.mnemonic: Think of 'Amul' butter. Adding Amul butter to a poorly cooked dish will instantly ameliorate (improve) the taste!

words.definition: (of a person) receptive to change; open / (of a thing) responsive to
words.example: The students were amenable to less homework. / The software is amenable to customization, and we can tailor it to our needs.
words.mnemonic: 'Amen' + 'able'. Someone who is always able to say 'Amen' (yes) to new proposals or changes.

words.definition: of the wrong time or era
words.example: Dreams of colonial conquest are anachronistic in today's modern world.
words.mnemonic: 'Ana' (wrong/against) + 'chron' (time). Using a bullock cart to commute in modern Ahmedabad instead of your Activa 3G would be highly anachronistic.

words.definition: bold; unafraid of risks / rude; exhibiting disrespect
words.example: Elon Musk incessantly pursues his audacious plan for a city on Mars. / His audacious remark during the meeting caught everyone off guard.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Audi'. Someone who boldly parks their luxury Audi right in the middle of a busy campus road is both daring and highly disrespectful.

words.example: Free market economies not only tolerate but reward avaricious behavior.
words.mnemonic: 'A' + 'vari' (very) + 'cious' (rich). A person who constantly wants to be *very rich* no matter what is avaricious.

words.example: The reviewer grew weary of banal, by-the-book action movies.
words.mnemonic: Ban-all. We should 'ban all' Bollywood movies that just copy the exact same boring, unoriginal storyline.

words.definition: harmless; having little effect / (of a person's character) kindhearted; nice
words.example: Despite causing some initial discomfort, the process is benign. / Despite his imposing stature, he had a benign smile.
words.mnemonic: 'Be' + 'nine'. Think of a sweet 9-year-old child. They are harmless, gentle, and kindhearted.

words.example: John's brazen interruptions of his boss' speech were not received well.
words.mnemonic: Think of the metal 'Brass'. It is loud, flashy, and bold compared to subtle metals. Someone who is brazen has a loud, shameless personality.

words.example: The tabloid had an axe to grind and thus spread calumny daily.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Kaale Munh' (black face in Hindi). Spreading false rumors to blacken someone's face and destroy their reputation.

words.example: Honesty is important, but don't be too candid in job interviews.
words.mnemonic: Think of 'Candy'. If you give a kid a piece of candy, they will drop all lies and be completely honest and sweet with you.

words.example: The entire country castigated the governor for his ill-timed statement.
words.mnemonic: 'Cast' + 'gate'. If you improperly 'cast' a metal part in the foundry lab and cause a defect, the professor will scold you all the way to the campus 'gate'.

words.definition: (of speech) hostile and bitter / (of a chemical) corrosive; acidic
words.example: Reality TV thrives on conflict and caustic discourse between characters. / Care should be taken when handling the caustic substance.
words.mnemonic: Think of 'caustic soda'. It is a highly corrosive chemical. A person's speech can be just as biting and corrosive to someone's feelings.

words.example: It was difficult to construe the retreat as anything but a massive defeat.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Construct'. When reading a dense research paper, you have to 'construct' the meaning in your own mind to understand it.

words.example: Genuinely contrite, the man accepted his sentence without protest.
words.mnemonic: 'Count' + 'right'. A cashier miscounted the money, realized their mistake, and felt extremely guilty about not getting the count right.

words.example: Rome's roads are a convoluted mess.
words.mnemonic: Think of the 'volute' casing of a centrifugal pump. The spiral, twisted path makes the fluid flow complex, just like a convoluted argument is hard to follow.

words.example: To improve our mental health, let's try to not covet the possessions of others.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Covert' (secret). You covertly desire to have the top rank in your management entrance exams.

words.example: The army surrendered but not because the soldiers were craven.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Cave in'. A coward will quickly 'cave in' under the pressure of a difficult project rather than boldly facing it.

words.example: The mother encouraged her son to have decorum.
words.mnemonic: Think of 'Decor'. Just as beautiful decor makes a room look proper and presentable, decorum makes a person's behavior proper.

words.example: Jane's deft fingers navigate the 88 piano keys effortlessly.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Deaf'. Beethoven was deaf, yet he was incredibly deft (skilled) at composing masterpieces.

words.definition: hesitate or object; express doubt / hesitation
words.example: Most employees demurred when asked to undergo daily Covid testing. / The judge asked if there were any objections, and there was a brief demur from the defense attorney before proceeding.
words.mnemonic: Think of 'Damn U R wrong'. If you think someone's calculations are incorrect, you will object and demur.

words.definition: based on someone else's work (often used in a negative sense)
words.example: The film is derivative, but proudly so.
words.mnemonic: Just like in calculus where a derivative is mathematically derived from a base function, a derivative artwork isn't original; it's just copied from something else.

words.example: The land was thoroughly desiccated.
words.mnemonic: 'Desi' + 'cake'. A traditional dry Indian rusk (a desi cake) has absolutely all of its moisture baked out of it.

words.example: Her diatribe on the ills of society was too hostile to persuade anyone.
words.mnemonic: 'Dia' (two) + 'tribe'. When two warring tribes meet, their leaders launch into long, angry verbal attacks against each other.

words.example: People are understandably incredulous at the prospect of intergalactic travel.
words.mnemonic: 'In' (not) + 'credit'. You refuse to give 'credit' or belief to a wild story because it seems too impossible.

words.example: We long for the ingenuous days of our youth.
words.mnemonic: Looks a lot like 'Genuine'. A purely genuine, innocent person who is completely naive to the harsh realities of the world.

words.example: He relied on TV to abate his lonelinesss.
words.mnemonic: Think of a 'battery' (a-bate) losing its charge. Over time, the voltage and power will naturally abate.

words.example: Under enough pressure, many will abjure their principles.
words.mnemonic: 'Ab' (now) + 'jure' (jury). Swearing formally in front of a jury that you will reject and abandon your previous claims.

words.definition: differing from what is normal; atypical
words.example: Because the results are anomalous, further study is needed.
words.mnemonic: Contains 'anomaly'. Getting an efficiency greater than 100% in a thermodynamics lab is highly anomalous and indicates a calculation error.

words.example: The two generals harbored significant antipathy toward each other.
words.mnemonic: 'Anti' (against) + 'pathy' (feeling, like sympathy). You have strong feelings against something.

words.definition: comprehended by very few people; secret
words.example: The profession keeps things arcane to discourage newcomers.
words.mnemonic: 'Arre, can'. Arre, only a few people can understand this! Just like a highly complex MATLAB script written without any comments.

words.example: Military bootcamp is almost always an arduous experience.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Hard for us'. Pushing a broken-down Activa for 3 kilometers in the scorching Ahmedabad heat is an incredibly arduous task.

words.definition: naive; inexperienced / plain and simple / lacking skill
words.example: His artless comments about politics revealed how naive he was. / The room had an artless quality, furnished only with a simple table. / Her artless attempt at painting made it clear she had never held a brush before.
words.mnemonic: Without 'art' (skill or deception). A completely plain, unpolished piece of steel straight out of casting is artless.

words.definition: (of a lifestyle) very strict and lacking luxury / someone who adopts an ascetic lifestyle
words.example: Despite her vast wealth, she has adopted ascetic tendencies. / The ascetic lived in a small cell, devoid of luxuries.
words.mnemonic: Sounds a bit like 'Acidic'. An ascetic lifestyle is harsh and strict, like an acidic diet with absolutely no sweet luxuries.

words.definition: decrease a negative feeling / fulfill a certain desire
words.example: The prime minister's speech assuaged the fears of the population. / Only a buffet could assuage his appetite.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Aansoo' (tears) + 'wash'. Washing away someone's tears helps to comfort and assuage their grief.

words.example: Her excessive blinking betrayed her nervousness.
words.mnemonic: 'Be' + 'tray'. Accidentally dropping a tray of precision instruments in the manufacturing lab will betray the fact that your hands were shaking.

words.example: Hoping to escape the city, the couple opted for a bucolic Airbnb.
words.mnemonic: Think of a 'Bullock' cart. A peaceful village scene with bullock carts and green fields represents a bucolic lifestyle.

words.example: Because of technological advancements, crop yields have burgeoned.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Burger'. If you eat a lot of burgers every day, your waistline will burgeon rapidly.

words.example: The parents hated the drumkit because of its cacophonous racket.
words.mnemonic: 'Kaka' (uncle) + 'phone'. Your Kaka shouting loudly on his phone on a crowded train creates a highly cacophonous environment.

words.definition: treat like a god; deeply venerate
words.example: The later Roman empire began canonizing its leaders.
words.mnemonic: Think of a 'Canon' camera. When the paparazzi shoot photos of you with their Canon cameras everywhere you go, society has canonized you as a superstar.

words.example: The senator was publicly censured for her vile remarks.
words.mnemonic: Think of the film 'Censor' board. They heavily criticize and censure scenes they strongly disapprove of.

words.definition: using deception to attain a certain goal
words.example: His masterful chicanery allowed the realization of his political goals.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Shikari' (hunter). A hunter uses traps, camouflage, and chicanery to deceive and catch their prey.

words.example: In times of conflict, citizens must coalesce and rally around a common cause.
words.mnemonic: 'Coal' + 'less'. To survive using *less coal*, different factories must coalesce and pool their energy resources together.

words.example: Known for his cogent essays, the author has considerable influence.
words.mnemonic: 'Co' (together) + 'gent' (gentlemen). When smart gentlemen sit together to draft a business plan, the resulting document is very clear, logical, and cogent.

words.definition: persuasive and clear / attracting interest; fascinating
words.example: The argument was not compelling due to its logical fallacies. / The author's compelling storytelling had readers hooked from the first chapter.
words.mnemonic: 'Come' + 'spell'. A presentation so good that it forces you to come over and leaves you spellbound.

words.definition: argue a certain point of view; assert / compete / struggle or attempt to overcome something
words.example: The researchers contend that society will collapse by 2050. / Several teams will contend for the championship. / She contended with chronic illness her entire life.
words.mnemonic: 'Kaun' (who) + 'trend'. Arguing with rivals over who will trend on social media is contending for the top spot.

words.example: The once copious supply of used cars dried up quickly during the pandemic.
words.mnemonic: 'Copy' + 'us'. If the printer makes a thousand copies for us, we will have a copious amount of paper.

words.definition: (of a city) highly diverse / (of a person) sophisticated and urbane
words.example: New York City is arguably the most cosmopolitan metropolis in the world. / His cosmopolitan demeanor made him the perfect diplomat.
words.mnemonic: 'Cosmo' means universe. A truly cosmopolitan B-school cohort has brilliant minds gathered from all over the universe.

words.example: In certain cultures, it's important to treat the elderly with marked deference.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Reference'. You naturally show a lot of deference and respect when speaking to the professor who wrote you a stellar reference letter for your master's program.

words.example: The teacher's desultory instruction did little to inspire the students.
words.mnemonic: 'De' + 'result'. Approaching your 6th-semester coursework with a desultory attitude will definitely de-grade your final results.

words.example: University forced John to come out of his diffident shell.
words.mnemonic: 'Difficult' to be 'confident'. Someone who finds it difficult to assert themselves in group discussions is diffident.

words.definition: slow and unhurried; meant to stall
words.example: The filibuster is a dilatory measure designed to impede Congressional operations.
words.mnemonic: 'Delay' + 'tory' (story). When a contractor feeds you a fabricated story just to delay a project, they are using dilatory tactics.

words.definition: speak evasively or dishonestly; avoid taking a stance
words.example: Seasoned sports stars recognize it's best to equivocate when asked pointed questions.
words.mnemonic: 'Equal' + 'vocal'. Being equally vocal in favor of both sides of an argument so that nobody actually knows what your real stance is.

words.example: The inflammatory rhetoric is designed to polarize the populace.
words.mnemonic: Think of the North and South 'Poles'. Pushing people's opinions so far apart that they end up on opposite ends of the earth.

words.definition: carelessly wasteful; extravagant / a person who is excessively wasteful
words.example: Prodigal resource extraction does not benefit an economy long-term. / After years of reckless spending and estrangement, the prodigal returned home.
words.mnemonic: 'Proud' + 'gal/guy'. A proud guy who throws his money around on luxury items instead of investing smartly in TATA Steel or HDFC is being prodigal.

words.example: The professor's verbose lectures were surprisingly entertaining.
words.mnemonic: 'Verb' + 'boss'. A manager or boss who uses way too many verbs and drawn-out sentences in a simple email.

words.example: He has abstained from alcohol for over three years.
words.mnemonic: 'Ab' (now) + 'stain'. To avoid getting a stain on your pristine white shirt, you must abstain from eating messy street food.

words.example: Roman leaders thrived on approbation from the masses.
words.mnemonic: 'Approve' + 'Probation'. When you perform well and pass your job probation period, you receive formal approval and approbation from your manager.

words.example: We must cherish the memories of youth.
words.mnemonic: Think of a sweet 'Cherry' on a cake. You save it for last because you greatly love and cherish it.

words.example: We've yet to find evidence that corroborates our hypothesis.
words.mnemonic: 'Co' (together) + 'robot'. In modern manufacturing, a co-bot works alongside humans to support and corroborate their assembly efforts.

words.example: The politicians' disparate viewpoints could not be reconciled.
words.mnemonic: 'Dis' + 'part'. If you grab two completely different mechanical parts from the bin, they will not fit together because they are disparate.

words.example: To improve in a specific field, simply emulate the actions of the experts.
words.mnemonic: Think of a software 'emulator' on your computer that perfectly imitates an old gaming console. To succeed as an industrialist, you emulate the habits of top CEOs.

words.definition: sap the energy out of someone; fatigue
words.example: The coach took pride in enervating the players.
words.mnemonic: 'Energy' + 'evaporate'. Standing near a hot blast furnace during a summer afternoon in Ahmedabad will cause your energy to evaporate, leaving you completely enervated.

words.example: It was an ephemeral thought that quickly melted away.
words.mnemonic: 'E' (electronic) + 'fame'. Going viral on social media brings electronic fame that is incredibly short-lasting and ephemeral.

words.example: The leader's fervid pleas did not go unheard.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Fever'. When you have a burning fever of passion for a new business idea, you work on it with fervid energy.

words.definition: tending to talk too much; wordy
words.example: Because she's garrulous, this meeting will take forever.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Gargle'. Someone who never stops talking sounds like they are endlessly gargling words.

words.example: His incendiary words did little to appease their anger.
words.mnemonic: Think of an 'Incendiary' bomb designed to start fires. Incendiary remarks are meant to start fiery, explosive conflicts.

words.definition: damaging; hostile in manner or tone
words.example: The entire affair has been inimical to our interests.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Enemy'. An enemy's actions are always hostile and inimical to your success.

words.example: The professor intimated there would be a test next week.
words.mnemonic: When two friends are very 'intimate' (close), they don't need to speak loudly; they can just subtly intimate their thoughts to one another.

words.example: Nothing invigorates the body like a good night's rest.
words.mnemonic: 'In' + 'Vigor'. To inject vigor and energy back into a system. A hot cup of chai can invigorate you during an all-night study session.

words.example: You can take some medicine to mitigate the pain.
words.mnemonic: 'Mitti' (mud) + 'gate'. Building a gate out of mitti (sandbags) helps to mitigate the damage caused by heavy floodwaters.

words.example: Fax machines are mostly obsolete now.
words.mnemonic: 'Ob' + 'so' + 'late'. Oh, this machine is so late to the modern era that it is completely outmoded and obsolete.

words.definition: unclear; hard to comprehend / not able to be seen through
words.example: The scientist's opaque explanation left the audience more confused than enlightened. / The visual evidence is not reliable given the windows were so opaque.
words.mnemonic: 'O' + 'pack'. A heavily packed, solid cardboard box is opaque; light cannot pass through it, and you cannot comprehend what is inside.

words.example: Coconuts, palm trees, and beaches are paradigmatic tropical things.
words.mnemonic: Contains 'Paradigm' (a standard model). A perfectly designed monocoque chassis serves as a paradigmatic example for other engineering students to follow.

words.example: The pedantic instruction annoys and frustrates the students.
words.mnemonic: Think of a 'Pandit' who is incredibly strict about following every single minor detail and rule during a puja ceremony.

words.definition: calm; free from activity or irritation
words.example: Most of us seek placid settings on our vacations.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Plastic'. A smooth sheet of plastic has no ripples or waves; it is perfectly calm and placid.

words.example: His work is usually polemical, but this latest is surprisingly neutral.
words.mnemonic: 'Pole' + 'Mic'. Picture an angry politician climbing up a street pole with a mic to shout highly controversial opinions.

words.definition: cause to happen / done quickly or suddenly
words.example: Religious tension precipitated the conflict. / Her precipitate decision to quit her job left her unemployed and full of regret.
words.mnemonic: Think of 'Precipitation' (rain). A heavy downpour happens suddenly and fast, and it can quickly precipitate a flood in the streets.

words.example: James claims to possess profundity, but his advice often comes from Google.
words.mnemonic: Related to 'Profound'. A senior professor at PDEU with decades of research experience speaks with great profundity.

words.definition: able to correctly predict the future
words.example: If you collect enough predictions, one will inevitably seem prophetic.
words.mnemonic: Derived from 'Prophet'. If your analysis of TATA Steel shares successfully predicts a massive breakout, your friends will call your skills prophetic.

words.example: Not saving for retirement is simply not prudent.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Student'. A good 6th-semester student is highly prudent about balancing their GRE prep time with their mechanical engineering lab work.

words.definition: highly concerned with details or manners
words.example: The chef is very punctilious in selecting ingredients.
words.mnemonic: Contains 'Punctual'. Someone who is obsessively punctual down to the exact second is usually very punctilious about all other details as well.

words.example: The book is far too recondite to be enjoyed by most.
words.mnemonic: Think of 'Recon' (military reconnaissance). Recon missions operate in the shadows, gathering complex, hidden information that is recondite to normal citizens.

words.definition: careful with details / possessing moral integrity
words.example: He was scrupulous in his research, double-checking every source. / She was scrupulous in her business dealings, always prioritizing honesty.
words.mnemonic: Think of 'Sculptor'. A master sculptor must be incredibly careful and scrupulous with every single chisel stroke.

words.example: A trip to the spa is normally a tranquil experience.
words.mnemonic: 'Train' + 'Pill'. If you take a sleeping pill during a long train journey, your ride will be deeply peaceful and tranquil.

words.definition: go back and forth with one's view
words.example: An expert politician, he vacillated when asked an uncomfortable question.
words.mnemonic: Rhymes with 'Oscillate'. An oscillating fan swings back and forth, just like a person who vacillates between two difficult choices.

words.example: She sat in the corner, aloof from the rest of us.
words.mnemonic: Think of 'A' (no) + 'roof'. Someone who prefers to stand outside alone in the rain rather than under the roof with everyone else is acting aloof.

words.example: The clangor of the construction site interrupted our conversation.
words.mnemonic: Contains 'Clang'. The loud *clang* of heavy metal parts dropping onto the floor of the PDEU manufacturing lab creates a massive clangor.

words.definition: adhering to established practices or accepted standards
words.example: He has very conventional views about education.
words.mnemonic: Think of conventional machining processes like turning and milling, as opposed to modern, unconventional ones like EDM.

words.definition: prove a belief or idea false
words.example: The scientist debunked the myth that vaccines cause autism.
words.mnemonic: 'De' + 'bunk'. If you try to 'bunk' your college lab with a fake excuse, a strict professor will quickly debunk your lie.

words.definition: exceptionally small in size or stature
words.example: Her diminutive stature did not affect her powerful voice.
words.mnemonic: Related to 'Diminish' (make smaller). A 1:10 scale model of a prefabricated cement building is a diminutive version of the real structure.

words.definition: able to be recognized or understood
words.example: There was no discernible change in his attitude.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Discover' + 'able'. When looking at a stress-strain curve for mild steel, the yield point is easily discernible.

words.example: Her silence on the issue was enigmatic.
words.mnemonic: The 'Enigma' machine was used in WWII to create highly secret, complex codes that were completely enigmatic to the enemy.

words.example: He became estranged from his family after moving overseas.
words.mnemonic: 'Extra' + 'Range'. You have put extra range (distance) between yourself and your old friends, making you essentially strangers.

words.definition: excessive, especially in terms of cost
words.example: His extravagant lifestyle was unsustainable in the long term.
words.mnemonic: Spending thousands of rupees on fancy daily coffees when you should be saving up for a Master's in Management abroad is highly extravagant.

words.example: His fanciful ideas about living on Mars were amusing.
words.mnemonic: 'Fancy' + 'full'. A business plan for tyre recycling that assumes raw materials will magically teleport to the factory for free is completely fanciful.

words.definition: lacking in seriousness or importance; silly / (of a person) not taking things seriously
words.example: She dismissed his comment as frivolous. / Believing that, in the end, nothing really matters, he's adopted a frivolous attitude.
words.mnemonic: 'Free' + 'voice'. Using your voice freely to joke around during a serious engineering lab exam is a frivolous way to act.

words.example: The population of the city is quite heterogeneous.
words.mnemonic: 'Hetero' (different) + 'geneous' (kind). A composite material like reinforced concrete is highly heterogeneous compared to a pure block of aluminum.

words.definition: having an arrogant or domineering manner
words.example: His imperious demeanor was off-putting to his colleagues.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Emperor'. An emperor often acts imperious, loudly bossing around his subjects without listening to their opinions.

words.definition: disrespectful or inappropriate / irrelevant or unrelated to something
words.example: His impertinent behavior at the meeting shocked everyone. / Employees kept bringing up impertinent issues at the meeting.
words.mnemonic: 'Im' (not) + 'pertinent' (relevant). A junior intern confidently telling a veteran industrialist how to run their personal life is both irrelevant and impertinent.

words.definition: infringing on other's privacy / spreading rapidly and causing harm
words.example: The invasive surveillance techniques used by the government were criticized. / The kudzu vine is an invasive species in the southern United States.
words.mnemonic: Related to 'Invade'. Destructive testing methods are invasive because they permanently alter or break the mechanical component being tested.

words.example: She was irresolute about her decision to move abroad.
words.mnemonic: 'Ir' (not) + 'resolute' (resolved). You have no resolution. Staring at INSEAD and HEC Paris brochures but being irresolute about which to apply to.

words.example: His efforts to protect the environment were laudable.
words.mnemonic: Contains 'Laud' (praise). Successfully deriving the governing ODEs for tank drainage and coding them in MATLAB is a highly laudable achievement.

words.example: The manager's lax attitude towards safety was a cause for concern.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Relax'. A workshop supervisor who just wants to relax and ignores students not wearing safety shoes is being dangerously lax.

words.definition: treat someone or something as insignificant
words.example: They felt marginalized by society because of their religious beliefs.
words.mnemonic: Pushing someone to the 'margin' (edge) of the paper, making them feel like a completely insignificant part of the team.

words.example: The actor performed the role with panache.
words.mnemonic: Think of an old-school Bollywood hero with a great mustache, chewing paan, and delivering his dialogue with pure panache.

words.example: The plodding pace of the meeting made him feel sleepy.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Plotting' along. A heavy tractor plotting through a muddy field moves at a very plodding pace compared to your Activa 3G on a highway.

words.example: His speech was prosaic, devoid of any rhetorical flourish.
words.mnemonic: 'Prose' instead of poetry. A standard, dry engineering lab report is usually quite prosaic and entirely lacking in creativity.

words.definition: serving to correct or cure something
words.example: She was placed in a remedial reading class to improve her skills.
words.mnemonic: From the word 'Remedy' (cure). If you fail your machine design midterm, you might have to take remedial classes to fix your grades.

words.example: The audience grew restive as the presentation continued.
words.mnemonic: 'Rest' + 'ive'. Paradoxically, it means wanting to break *out* of resting. Waiting for an hour in a crowded Ahmedabad office makes people highly restive.

words.example: There have been sporadic outbreaks of the disease.
words.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Spores' blowing in the wind. They don't fall in a steady pattern; they land randomly and sporadically.

words.example: The campaign aims to stigmatize smoking in public areas.
words.mnemonic: From 'Stigma' (a mark of shame). In the past, people would stigmatize failed business ventures, but modern industrialists know failure is just part of the process.

words.example: The constant criticism served to undermine her confidence.
words.mnemonic: 'Under' + 'mine'. Literally digging a coal mine directly underneath a prefabricated building to slowly and subtly weaken its foundation.

words.example: He was utterly convinced of his own success.
words.mnemonic: When a complex Python code to calculate vibrations runs perfectly on the very first try, you are left utterly speechless.

words.example: After the long journey, they were weary and ready to rest.
words.mnemonic: After you 'wear' out your tyres traveling 14 km a day on your scooter, you feel completely weary from the bumpy ride.

words.example: She was a zealous advocate for human rights.
words.mnemonic: Contains 'Zeal'. To score a 320+ on the GRE and secure admission into a top management program, you must be absolutely zealous about your preparation.
